walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Number Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's set up The Mind! First, find the deck of one hundred numbered cards, with values from one to one hundred. Give them a good shuffle. These are the only cards you will be playing with during the game.

walkthrough.title: Set the Level
walkthrough.text: Place the Level one card in the center of the table. This tells everyone which level you are on. You will advance through the levels as you succeed. In a two-player game, you play through twelve levels. In a three-player game, ten levels. In a four-player game, eight levels.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Lives and Throwing Stars
walkthrough.text: Now set out your lives and throwing stars. Your team starts with lives equal to the number of players. So four players means four lives. You also start with one throwing star. Place these cards face up near the level card where everyone can see them.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Cards
walkthrough.text: Deal one card face down to each player for Level one. On Level two, each player will get two cards, and so on. Look at your card but do not show it to anyone and do not say anything about it. The golden rule of The Mind is no communication about your cards. Ready to sync up? Let's play.

summary.title: Shuffle the Number Cards
bullets: Shuffle the 100 numbered cards (1-100)
This is the only deck used in the game

summary.title: Set the Level
bullets: Place Level 1 card in center
2 players: 12 levels, 3 players: 10 levels, 4 players: 8 levels

summary.title: Prepare Lives and Throwing Stars
bullets: Lives = player count (4 players → 4 lives)
Start with 1 throwing star
Place face up near level card

summary.title: Deal the Cards
bullets: Level 1: deal 1 card per player
Level N: deal N cards per player
No communication about cards — the golden rule

walkthrough.title: The Core Concept
walkthrough.text: The Mind is a cooperative game where everyone must play their numbered cards in ascending order, but there are no turns. Anyone can play a card at any time. The catch is that you cannot talk, signal, or communicate about your cards in any way. You must develop a shared sense of timing with your teammates and feel when the moment is right.

walkthrough.title: The Focus Ritual
walkthrough.text: Before each level begins, all players place a hand flat on the table. This is the concentration ritual. It signals that everyone is focused and ready. Once all hands are down and the group feels synchronized, remove your hands and the level begins. This brief pause helps the team reset their mental clock.

walkthrough.title: Playing Cards
walkthrough.text: There are no turns. When you feel the time is right, play your lowest card face up on the center pile. The cards across all players must be played in ascending numerical order. If you hold a three and a forty-five, you must play the three before the forty-five. But you also need to wait for anyone else who might have a card lower than yours.

walkthrough.title: Errors and Losing Lives
walkthrough.text: If someone plays a card and another player still has a lower card in their hand, that is an error. The player with the lower card must immediately reveal and discard it. The team loses one life for each error. If you run out of lives, the game is over and everyone loses.

walkthrough.title: Throwing Stars
walkthrough.text: At any time before a card is played, any player can raise their hand to propose using a throwing star. If all players agree by raising their hands, each player discards their lowest card face up. This gives everyone information and can clear dangerous situations. Throwing stars are rare and valuable, so use them wisely.

walkthrough.title: Completing a Level
walkthrough.text: When all cards for the current level have been played in order without running out of lives, you have completed the level. Congratulations! Advance the level card. Certain levels reward the team with a bonus life or an extra throwing star. Shuffle all the cards, deal the new amount, and start the next level.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing
walkthrough.text: If your team completes all the levels, you win! That is Level twelve for two players, Level ten for three players, or Level eight for four players. If you lose all your lives at any point, the game is over and you have lost. There is no scoring. It is a pure pass-or-fail cooperative experience.

bullets: Cooperative: play cards 1-100 in ascending order
No turns — anyone can play at any time
No communication about cards — feel the timing

walkthrough.title: Welcome to The Mind
walkthrough.text: We will walk through the first three levels with four players. Even if you have a different player count, follow along to feel how the game flows. The key thing to remember: no talking, no signals, just shared intuition.

walkthrough.title: Level One Setup
walkthrough.text: Place the Level one card in the center. You have four lives and one throwing star. Shuffle the deck and deal one card to each player. Player one has a thirty-seven. Player two has an eighty-two. Player three has a twelve. Player four has a fifty-five. Nobody knows anyone else's card.

walkthrough.title: The Focus Ritual
walkthrough.text: Everyone places a hand on the table. Take a breath. Feel the group energy settle. When it feels right, lift your hands. The level has begun. Now everyone stares at the center of the table, waiting, feeling the moment.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Plays First
walkthrough.text: Player three has the twelve. That is a low number, so Player three should feel confident playing it early. After a few seconds, Player three places the twelve face up in the center. Good instinct. Nobody had anything lower, so no error.

walkthrough.title: The Waiting Game
walkthrough.text: Now there is a pause. Player one has thirty-seven but is not sure if someone has something between twelve and thirty-seven. After a longer pause, Player one places the thirty-seven. Still safe. Player four waits a bit more, then plays fifty-five. Finally, Player two plays eighty-two. Level one complete!

walkthrough.title: Level Two: Two Cards Each
walkthrough.text: Advance to Level two. Shuffle and deal two cards to each player. Player one has twenty-three and sixty-one. Player two has eight and forty-four. Player three has thirty-one and seventy-nine. Player four has fifteen and ninety-two. Now things get interesting with eight cards to order.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Playing Too Fast
walkthrough.text: Player four has fifteen and feels it is low enough to play right away. They slam it down almost immediately. But wait, Player two had an eight! Player two reveals the eight, and the team loses one life. The lesson: even low numbers need a moment. Give the group a few seconds to settle before playing anything, especially at the start of a level.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Slow Down
walkthrough.text: The team has three lives left. No need to panic. Take the focus ritual again, put hands on the table, and recalibrate. Player two discards the eight, and the level continues from fifteen. The team plays more carefully now, pausing longer between cards. Twenty-three, thirty-one, forty-four, sixty-one, seventy-nine, ninety-two. Level two complete despite the error.

walkthrough.title: Level Three: Three Cards Each
walkthrough.text: Player one has five, forty-one, and sixty-seven. Player two has twenty-two, thirty-nine, and eighty-eight. Player three has three, fifty, and seventy-three. Player four has nineteen, forty-three, and ninety-five.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring the Throwing Star
walkthrough.text: Player one has a five and Player three has a three. These are very close together. Player three hesitates because they are not sure if someone has an even lower card. This is a perfect time for a throwing star! But nobody raises their hand to propose it. Player three eventually plays the three, and luckily Player one had five, not two. But if they had been even closer, the error would have cost another life.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: When to Use Throwing Stars
walkthrough.text: Use a throwing star when you sense tension in the group, especially when you hold cards that are close together, like forty-one and forty-three. If multiple players seem hesitant, raise your hand. Everyone discards their lowest card face up, revealing crucial information. Do not hoard throwing stars. They are most valuable early in a level when the most cards are in play.

walkthrough.title: Finishing Level Three
walkthrough.text: The team settles into a rhythm. Three, five, nineteen, twenty-two, thirty-nine, forty-one, forty-three, fifty, sixty-seven, seventy-three, eighty-eight, ninety-five. All twelve cards played in perfect order. Level three complete! The group is starting to develop their shared mental clock.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have made it through the first three levels of The Mind. You have experienced the focus ritual, learned that playing too fast causes errors, and discovered when throwing stars can save the day. The game gets harder with each level, but your group's telepathic connection gets stronger too.

walkthrough.title: Before You Sync Up Again
walkthrough.text:
Before you continue to higher levels, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for timing strategies and reading your teammates.

Second, reference the Appendix for level rewards and player-count details.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

bullets: Scripted tutorial with 4 players, first 3 levels
Follow along with any player count
No talking, no signals — just shared intuition

summary.title: Before You Sync Up Again
bullets: Check out General Tips → timing strategies
Reference the Appendix → level rewards and player counts
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Develop a Mental Clock
walkthrough.text: The most important skill in The Mind is developing an internal sense of timing. Many players use a mental counting technique: wait roughly one second for every number on your card. If you hold a twenty, wait about twenty seconds after the level starts. If you hold a seventy, wait about seventy seconds. This is not a perfect system, but it gives you a baseline that the whole group can calibrate around.

walkthrough.title: Low Cards Go Fast
walkthrough.text: Cards in the one to ten range should be played almost immediately. There is very little chance someone has a lower card, and waiting too long with a low card creates confusion. The group expects low numbers to come out right away. If nobody plays in the first few seconds, that itself is information. It tells you there are no very low cards this round.

walkthrough.title: Read the Energy
walkthrough.text: While you cannot communicate about your cards, body language is a natural part of human interaction. If someone looks tense or anxious, they probably have a card they want to play. If someone looks relaxed, they probably have high numbers. You are not signaling. You are simply being human. Pay attention to the energy at the table and use it to refine your timing.

walkthrough.title: Throwing Star Timing
walkthrough.text: Use throwing stars when you have cards that are dangerously close together. If you hold a forty-one and a forty-three, there is a high chance someone else has forty-two. That is a throwing star moment. Also use them when the group seems stuck and nobody wants to play. The information from discarding lowest cards can break the gridlock.

walkthrough.title: Accept the Losses
walkthrough.text: You will lose lives. It is part of the game. Do not get frustrated when someone plays out of order. Instead, use the error as calibration data. If Player three played a forty but you had a thirty-eight, now you both know that your timing was slightly off. Adjust your mental clocks accordingly. Each error makes the group better.

walkthrough.title: The Power of Group Experience
walkthrough.text: The Mind gets dramatically easier the more you play with the same group. Your shared mental clock becomes more accurate with each game. Groups that play regularly develop an almost telepathic sense of timing. If your first few games are rough, stick with it. The magic of The Mind is in the journey from fumbling to flowing.

summary.title: Develop a Mental Clock
bullets: Wait ~1 second per number on your card
Card 20 = wait ~20 seconds; card 70 = wait ~70 seconds
Not perfect, but gives the group a shared baseline

summary.title: Low Cards Go Fast
bullets: Cards 1-10: play almost immediately
Waiting with a low card creates confusion
No play in first few seconds = no very low cards

summary.title: Read the Energy
bullets: Tense body language → someone wants to play
Relaxed → probably high numbers
Natural reactions give clues (not signaling)

summary.title: Throwing Star Timing
bullets: Use when you hold close cards (e.g., 41 and 43)
Use when the group is stuck and nobody wants to play
Revealed lowest cards break gridlock

summary.title: Accept the Losses
bullets: Errors are calibration data, not failures
Use them to adjust your mental clocks
Each error makes the group better over time

summary.title: The Power of Group Experience
bullets: The Mind gets much easier with the same group
Shared mental clock improves with each game
First games are rough — stick with it for the magic

walkthrough.title: Calibrating the Group Clock
walkthrough.text: Advanced groups refine their mental clock beyond the simple one-second-per-number approach. Pay attention to the pace at which cards were played in previous levels. If the group tends to play faster, speed up your own counting. If they tend to play slower, slow down. The goal is to match the group's natural rhythm, not to impose your own.

walkthrough.title: The Gap Principle
walkthrough.text: When multiple cards are played in quick succession, the group has used up the "low zone." After a burst of cards, there is usually a gap. If you have a card in the middle range and nobody has played for a while, the pause itself tells you that remaining cards are high. Use the length of pauses between plays as information about where the next card likely falls.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Throwing Star Use
walkthrough.text: In higher levels, throwing stars become more powerful because each player discards their lowest card. With four players on Level seven, a single throwing star reveals four cards and removes four potential conflicts. Use them proactively at the start of a level when you hold a cluster of cards in a tight range. Do not save all your throwing stars for emergencies.

walkthrough.title: Managing Multi-Card Hands
walkthrough.text: On higher levels, you have many cards. Focus only on your lowest card at any given moment. Do not think about your whole hand. Once your lowest card is played, shift attention to the next lowest. This simplifies the mental load and keeps your timing sharp. If you try to track all seven cards simultaneously, you will freeze up.

walkthrough.title: Recovery After an Error
walkthrough.text: After an error, the discarded cards give everyone new information. Use this to recalibrate. If the error revealed a thirty-six and a forty, you now know the next card in play is above forty. Take a beat, do the focus ritual again, and resume with the new information factored in. Groups that recover well lose fewer additional lives.

walkthrough.title: The Blind Variant
walkthrough.text: For the ultimate challenge, try playing cards face down instead of face up. In this variant, you only discover errors after the level ends when you flip the pile over and check the order. This removes real-time feedback entirely and forces the group to trust their timing completely. It is brutally hard but incredibly satisfying when you succeed.

summary.title: Calibrating the Group Clock
bullets: Match the group's natural pace, don't impose your own
Observe previous levels' tempo and adjust
Speed up or slow down based on group tendencies

summary.title: The Gap Principle
bullets: Quick burst of cards → low zone is used up
Long pause after a burst → remaining cards are high
Pause length = information about next card range

summary.title: Strategic Throwing Star Use
bullets: Higher levels: 1 star removes 4 conflicts (4 players)
Use proactively when you hold tight card clusters
Don't hoard — early use prevents cascading errors

summary.title: Managing Multi-Card Hands
bullets: Focus only on your current lowest card
Once played, shift to next lowest
Don't track all 7 cards at once — you'll freeze

summary.title: Recovery After an Error
bullets: Discarded cards reveal new info — use it

Good recovery prevents cascading life losses

entries.definition: The deck of one hundred cards numbered from one to one hundred. These are the only playing cards in the game. Shuffled and dealt at the start of each level.
A card placed in the center indicating the current level number. On Level N, each player receives N cards. Advance the card after completing each level.
Represents the team's remaining lives. Lose one life per error. If all lives are lost, the game ends in defeat. Starting lives equal the player count.
A special resource the team shares. When used, each player discards their lowest card face up. The team starts with one throwing star and may earn more at certain levels.
The core rule of The Mind: all cards played to the center pile must go from low to high across all players. Breaking this order is an error that costs a life.

Occurs when a card is played while another player still holds a lower-numbered card. The lower card is discarded and one life is lost. Multiple lower cards from multiple players can be revealed simultaneously.
Players may not talk about, signal, or otherwise communicate information about their cards. The concentration gesture (hand on table) only means 'I am ready' and is not a signal about card values.
Placing a hand flat on the table before a level begins. This signals readiness and focus, not card information. All players must participate before the level starts.
Certain levels grant the team a bonus life or an extra throwing star upon completion. Check the level card or rulebook for which levels provide rewards.
Any player can raise their hand to propose using a throwing star. All players must agree by also raising their hands. If anyone declines, the star is not used.
An informal timing technique where players wait approximately one second per number on their card before playing it. Not an official rule, but a widely used strategy.
An optional variant where cards are played face down. Errors are only discovered at the end of the level when the pile is checked. Extremely difficult challenge mode.

Two players play through twelve levels, three players through ten levels, and four players through eight levels. Starting lives equal the player count. Fewer players means more levels but fewer simultaneous cards per level.
